topics.title: Design Patterns in Java
topics.description: Design patterns are proven, reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. Introduced by the Gang of Four (GoF), they are grouped into three categories: Creational (object creation), Structural (object composition), and Behavioral (object interaction). Patterns are not code you copy — they are templates for solving design problems. Understanding them helps you recognise the same structure in frameworks like Spring (Singleton, Proxy, Template Method), Java itself (Iterator, Observer, Factory), and your own codebase.
topics.story: Think of design patterns like architectural blueprints for buildings. A blueprint for a hotel lobby tells you where the reception desk goes, where the lifts are, and how guests flow through — but you still choose the materials, colours, and exact dimensions. Similarly, a design pattern tells you how classes relate and what responsibilities each has — but you write the actual code. GoF patterns emerged from watching experienced architects build the same structures over and over. They extracted the recurring shapes and gave them names so future architects could say 'this should be a Strategy pattern' instead of explaining the entire design from scratch.
topics.analogy: Singleton = the CEO of a company — there's exactly one, everyone reaches them through the same door. Factory = a car factory that produces different car models (Sedan, SUV) — you ask the factory for 'a car', not 'new Sedan()'. Observer = a YouTube subscription — the channel (subject) notifies all subscribers when a video is published. Decorator = coffee customisation — start with plain coffee, add milk (decorator), add sugar (decorator) — each wrapper adds behaviour without changing the original. Strategy = GPS navigation — you choose the route strategy (fastest, shortest, avoid tolls) and swap it at runtime.
topics.memoryAnchor: CSB mnemonic — Creational (HOW objects are BORN): Singleton=one instance globally, Factory Method=let subclass decide the type, Builder=step-by-step construction, Prototype=clone existing. Structural (HOW objects CONNECT): Adapter=convert incompatible interface, Decorator=wrap to add behavior without subclassing, Proxy=control access, Facade=simplify complex subsystem. Behavioral (HOW objects TALK): Observer=event publish/subscribe, Strategy=swap algorithm at runtime, Command=encapsulate action as object, Template=skeleton with hooks. Interview top 5: Strategy (replaces if-else chains), Observer (event systems), Singleton (shared resource), Factory (decouple creation), Decorator (add features dynamically).
topics.firstPrinciples: Every pattern solves the same core tension: change. Code that never changes needs no patterns. Patterns exist because requirements change — new payment methods, new notification channels, new file formats. Open/Closed Principle: open for extension, closed for modification. Strategy lets you add algorithms without touching existing code. Observer lets you add listeners without touching the event source. Decorator lets you add features without subclassing. The pattern is the mechanism for absorbing change without rewriting working code.
topics.bruteForce: Without patterns: a single God class with 2000 lines, switch statements everywhere (switch(type) case 'VISA': ... case 'PAYPAL': ...), copy-paste duplication instead of template methods, tight coupling where changing one class breaks five others, no way to add features without modifying core classes. The result: fragile, untestable, unmaintainable code that developers fear to touch.
topics.optimizedApproach: Apply patterns judiciously — not every class needs a pattern. Red flags that indicate a pattern: (1) switch on type → Strategy or Factory, (2) need to add behaviour without subclassing → Decorator, (3) one object must notify many others → Observer, (4) complex object construction → Builder, (5) need to control access or add cross-cutting concerns → Proxy, (6) algorithm skeleton with variable steps → Template Method.
topics.whenToUse: Singleton: configuration, thread pools, caches — things that must be shared globally. Factory/Abstract Factory: when the exact type is determined at runtime or by configuration. Builder: when constructors have many optional parameters (>4). Decorator: adding logging, caching, validation without subclassing. Proxy: lazy loading, remote access, access control, AOP. Observer: event systems, MVC model-view sync, reactive programming. Strategy: payment processing, sorting algorithms, compression formats. Command: undo/redo, job queues, transaction scripts. Template Method: frameworks where you define the skeleton, users fill the hooks.

examples.title: Creational Patterns — Singleton, Factory Method, Builder
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":17,"lineCode":"if (instance == null) { synchronized (DatabaseConnection.class) {","variables":{"instance":"null"},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"First null check — no synchronisation overhead on subsequent calls. Only enters the synchronized block if instance is null."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"if (instance == null) { instance = new DatabaseConnection(...); }","variables":{"instance":"null"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Second null check inside synchronized block — prevents race condition where two threads both pass the first check before synchronisation. volatile ensures the write is visible to all threads immediately."},{"line":42,"lineCode":"return switch (type.toLowerCase()) { case \"email\" -> new EmailNotification(); ... }","variables":{"type":"email"},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Factory centralises creation — callers say what they want, not how to create it. Adding a new type only requires adding one case here, not changing every caller."},{"line":81,"lineCode":"HttpRequest req = new HttpRequest.Builder(\"https://api...\")","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Builder pattern: mandatory params in constructor (url), optional params via fluent methods. build() validates and constructs the immutable HttpRequest. Compare to: new HttpRequest(\"POST\", url, headers, body, 3000) — parameter order errors are impossible with Builder."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Singleton
      A[getInstance call 1] -->|instance null| B[synchronized block]
      B --> C[create instance]
      D[getInstance call 2] -->|instance not null| E[return existing]
    end
    subgraph Factory
      F[createNotification type] --> G{switch type}
      G -->|email| H[new EmailNotification]
      G -->|sms|   I[new SMSNotification]
      G -->|push|  J[new PushNotification]
    end
    subgraph Builder
      K[new Builder url] -->|method| L
      L -->|header| M
      M -->|body| N
      N -->|timeout| O
      O -->|build| P[HttpRequest immutable]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
SINGLETON (double-checked locking):
  if instance == null:
    synchronized:
      if instance == null:   ← second check
        instance = new T()
  return instance
  requires: volatile field

FACTORY METHOD:
  static T create(String type):
    switch type → new ConcreteT()
  caller never uses new — just create(type)

BUILDER:
  new Builder(mandatory).optional1(v).optional2(v).build()
  build() → validates + constructs immutable object
  inner Builder class with fluent setters returning this
examples.explanation: Singleton uses double-checked locking with a volatile field — volatile prevents the JVM from reordering the assignment before the constructor finishes. Factory Method decouples object creation from usage — the caller asks for a Notification, not a specific class. Builder solves the 'telescoping constructor' problem — instead of constructors with 8 parameters, you chain only the ones you need. All three are Spring fundamentals: Spring beans are Singleton-scoped by default, BeanFactory is a Factory, and many Spring APIs (MockMvcRequestBuilders, UriComponentsBuilder) use Builder.
examples.realWorldUse: Singleton: Spring's ApplicationContext, HikariCP connection pool, Logger instances (LoggerFactory.getLogger). Factory: Spring's BeanFactory.getBean(), JDBC DriverManager.getConnection(), Calendar.getInstance(). Builder: Java's StringBuilder, Stream.Builder, Lombok's @Builder annotation, Spring MockMvc request builders, Retrofit/OkHttp client builders.

examples.title: Structural Patterns — Adapter, Decorator, Proxy
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":22,"lineCode":"legacy.writeLog(\"[\" + level + \"] \" + message);","variables":{"level":"INFO","message":"Application started"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Adapter translates the ModernLogger.log(level, message) call into LegacyLogger.writeLog(combined_string). The caller uses ModernLogger, never knows LegacyLogger exists."},{"line":48,"lineCode":"public String process(String text) { return wrapped.process(text).trim(); }","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Decorator delegates to the wrapped component first, then adds its own behaviour. Calling PrefixDecorator.process() calls UpperCaseDecorator → TrimDecorator → PlainText, then unwraps back up the chain."},{"line":68,"lineCode":"if (cache.containsKey(key)) { return cache.get(key); }","variables":{"key":"user:42","cacheHit":true},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"Caching Proxy: second call for same key returns immediately from HashMap — O(1) vs the expensive DB call. Caller uses DataService interface — they cannot tell if they're talking to RealDataService or CachingProxy."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Adapter
      Caller -->|log level msg| Adapter
      Adapter -->|writeLog combined| LegacyLogger
    end
    subgraph Decorator_Chain
      Input[text] --> Trim
      Trim --> Upper
      Upper --> Prefix
      Prefix --> Output[>> HELLO WORLD]
    end
    subgraph Proxy
      C2[Caller] -->|fetchData key| P[CachingProxy]
      P -->|miss| Real[RealDataService DB]
      P -->|hit| Cache[(HashMap cache)]
      Real --> Cache
    end
examples.explanation: Adapter bridges incompatible interfaces — it's the 'plug adapter' of software. Decorator wraps objects in layers like an onion — each layer adds one behaviour, and you compose them at runtime rather than via a class explosion of subclasses (TrimmedUpperCasePrefixedText would require 2^n subclasses). Proxy implements the same interface as the real object — callers cannot tell the difference, making it perfect for transparent caching, access control, and lazy loading.
examples.realWorldUse: Adapter: Java's Arrays.asList() adapts arrays to List. Spring's HandlerAdapter adapts various controller types to a unified interface. Decorator: Java's BufferedReader wraps FileReader (I/O decorators). Spring Security's filter chain is a decorator chain. HttpServletRequestWrapper lets you decorate requests. Proxy: Spring AOP creates proxies for @Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async. Hibernate's lazy-loaded entities are proxies. Java's Proxy.newProxyInstance() creates dynamic proxies.

examples.title: Behavioral Patterns — Strategy, Observer, Command
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":32,"lineCode":"void setStrategy(SortStrategy<T> s) { this.strategy = s; }","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Strategy swap at runtime — the Sorter doesn't know or care which algorithm runs. Replace BubbleSort with QuickSort, MergeSort, or any new algorithm without touching Sorter. Open for extension, closed for modification."},{"line":50,"lineCode":"listeners.forEach(l -> l.onEvent(event));","variables":{"listenerCount":2},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Observer: the EventBus (subject) iterates all registered listeners and calls onEvent. Email and SMS handlers are completely decoupled from each other — adding a new listener (e.g., Push notification) requires zero changes to existing code."},{"line":65,"lineCode":"cmd.execute(); history.push(cmd);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Command pattern: execute the action AND store the command object on a stack. Undo = pop from stack and call undo(). The editor never knows what action was performed — it just calls execute() and undo() on the abstract Command interface."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Strategy
      Sorter -->|strategy.sort| BubbleSort
      Sorter -->|swap| JavaSort
    end
    subgraph Observer
      Publisher[EventBus.publish] --> Email[EmailListener]
      Publisher --> SMS[SMSListener]
      Publisher --> Push[PushListener add later]
    end
    subgraph Command
      Client -->|executeCommand| Editor
      Editor -->|push| Stack[(History Stack)]
      Client -->|undo| Stack
      Stack -->|pop.undo| Editor
    end
examples.explanation: Strategy encapsulates a family of algorithms behind an interface, making them interchangeable. The context delegates to the strategy — swapping algorithms without conditional logic. Observer decouples event producers from consumers — the subject never imports listener classes. Command wraps actions as objects — enabling undo/redo, queuing, logging, and transaction scripts. These three patterns appear everywhere in enterprise Java: Strategy in payment processors, Observer in Spring events, Command in Spring Batch step execution.
examples.realWorldUse: Strategy: Spring Security's AuthenticationManager selects an authentication strategy. Java's Comparator is a Strategy. Compression libraries (zip/gzip/brotli) use Strategy. Observer: Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher, Java Swing listeners, Kafka consumer groups, reactive streams. Command: Spring Batch's Step, Git commits (each is a reversible command), database transactions, job schedulers.

examples.title: Behavioral Patterns — Template Method, Chain of Responsibility, State
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"final void migrate() { connect(); extract(); transform(); load(); disconnect(); }","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Template Method: the base class defines the algorithm skeleton with final — subclasses CANNOT change the order. They only override the abstract hook methods (extract, transform, load). connect() and disconnect() have default implementations but can be overridden."},{"line":47,"lineCode":"protected boolean passToNext(int level, String req) { return next != null && next.handle(level, req); }","variables":{"hasNext":true},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Chain of Responsibility: each handler decides whether to process or pass. If a handler rejects (auth failure, rate limit), it returns false and the chain stops. If it approves, it passes to next. Order matters: Auth → RateLimit → Logging."},{"line":84,"lineCode":"void tick() { state.enter(); state = state.next(); }","variables":{"state":"RedState"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"State pattern: behaviour is fully delegated to the current state object. state.enter() prints the state message. state.next() returns the next state object — the TrafficLight never has if/switch statements. Adding a new state (BlinkingRed) requires only a new class."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Template_Method
      TM[migrate] --> C[connect base]
      C --> E[extract abstract]
      E --> T[transform abstract]
      T --> L[load abstract]
      L --> D[disconnect base]
    end
    subgraph Chain_of_Responsibility
      Req --> Auth{Auth OK?}
      Auth -->|yes| Rate{Rate OK?}
      Auth -->|no| REJECT1[Reject]
      Rate -->|yes| Log[Log + done]
      Rate -->|no| REJECT2[Reject]
    end
    subgraph State_Machine
      Red -->|next| Green
      Green -->|next| Yellow
      Yellow -->|next| Red
    end
examples.explanation: Template Method defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class and lets subclasses override specific steps — the sequence is fixed, only the content of steps varies. This is the core pattern of every framework: Spring's JdbcTemplate, AbstractController, AbstractBatchJob all define the skeleton and call your hooks. Chain of Responsibility passes a request along a chain of handlers — each handler decides to process or pass on. HTTP middleware, servlet filters, and Spring Security's filter chain use this. State removes conditionals by delegating behaviour to state objects — the context never has switch/if statements.
examples.realWorldUse: Template Method: JdbcTemplate.query() calls your RowMapper hook. AbstractList requires only get() and size(). JUnit lifecycle (@BeforeEach, @Test, @AfterEach) is template method. Chain of Responsibility: Spring Security's filter chain, Java servlet filters, logging handlers (console → file → remote). State: Order state machine (PENDING→PAID→SHIPPED→DELIVERED), TCP connection states, media player (PLAYING→PAUSED→STOPPED).

examples.title: Advanced Patterns — Composite, Facade, Flyweight, Prototype
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":27,"lineCode":"children.forEach(c -> c.render(indent + \"  \"));","variables":{"name":"Root","childCount":2},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Composite: Panel.render() calls render() on each child. If the child is a Panel (composite), it recursively renders its children. If it's a Button (leaf), it just prints. The caller treats both uniformly through UIComponent."},{"line":47,"lineCode":"void processVideo(String filename) { encoder, extractor, thumbGen, uploader }","variables":{"filename":"lecture.mp4"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Facade: the caller makes ONE call. Behind the scenes, 4 subsystem classes are orchestrated. The caller is completely shielded from complexity — if the encoding pipeline changes, only the Facade changes."},{"line":61,"lineCode":"return pool.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new CharacterGlyph(c, font, size));","variables":{"poolSize":1,"key":"H|Arial|12"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Flyweight: computeIfAbsent creates the glyph only if not in the pool. 'HelloHello' = 10 characters, but only 4 unique glyphs (H,e,l,o). The pool saves 60% object creation. Each RenderedChar holds position (extrinsic state) but shares the glyph (intrinsic state)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Composite_Tree
      Root --> Toolbar
      Root --> Content
      Toolbar --> Save[Button Save]
      Toolbar --> Load[Button Load]
      Content --> Submit[Button Submit]
    end
    subgraph Facade
      Client2 -->|processVideo| F[VideoFacade]
      F --> Encoder
      F --> AudioExtractor
      F --> ThumbnailGen
      F --> Uploader
    end
    subgraph Flyweight
      H1[H pos 0] -->|share| GH[(Glyph H)]
      H2[H pos 40] -->|share| GH
      E1[e pos 8] -->|share| GE[(Glyph e)]
    end
examples.explanation: Composite builds tree structures where individual objects and compositions are treated uniformly — perfect for UI hierarchies, file systems, and org charts. Facade provides a simplified entry point to a complex subsystem — used in every SDK and framework API. Flyweight shares common state across thousands of objects — a text editor rendering 100,000 characters needs only ~100 unique glyph objects. The key insight: separate shared intrinsic state (glyph shape) from unique extrinsic state (screen position).
examples.realWorldUse: Composite: Java's AWT/Swing component tree, HTML DOM (every element can contain other elements), JUnit 5 test suites contain test classes contain test methods. Facade: SLF4J is a facade over Log4j/Logback/JUL. Spring's JdbcTemplate is a facade over JDBC. REST clients (Feign, Retrofit) facade HTTP. Flyweight: Java's Integer.valueOf() caches -128..127. String interning. Java's Character class. Game engines sharing sprite textures among thousands of game objects.

problems.title: Thread-Safe Singleton
problems.description: Implement a thread-safe `AppConfig` Singleton that reads key-value properties from a `Map<String, String>`. It must be lazily initialised (created on first access, not at class load) and thread-safe. Provide `get(String key)` and `getOrDefault(String key, String def)` methods.
problems.hint1: Use `volatile static AppConfig instance` with double-checked locking in `getInstance()`.
problems.hint2: Or use the Enum singleton pattern — Java enums are inherently thread-safe singletons.
problems.hint3: Or use the Initialization-on-demand holder idiom: a private static inner class with the instance — the JVM guarantees its class loading is thread-safe.
problems.constraints: Must be lazily initialised and thread-safe.
problems.editorial: The Initialization-on-demand holder is the cleanest singleton in Java: the JVM guarantees class initialisation is single-threaded, so Holder.INSTANCE is created exactly once, lazily (when getInstance() is first called), and without explicit synchronisation. Double-checked locking with volatile also works but is more verbose. Never use Enum singleton for a class that needs to extend another class.

problems.title: Factory Registry — Plugin System
problems.description: Implement a `NotificationFactory` that supports registering new notification types at runtime (plugin pattern). `register(String type, Supplier<Notification> factory)` adds a new type. `create(String type)` creates one. Default types: EMAIL, SMS, PUSH. Show how to add WEBHOOK at runtime.
problems.hint1: Use a `Map<String, Supplier<Notification>>` as the registry.
problems.hint2: `register(type, factory)` puts into the map. `create(type)` gets and calls `factory.get()`.
problems.hint3: Initialize the map with default types in a static block or constructor.

testCases.output:
Email: Welcome!
Webhook: Order shipped
Caught: Unknown notification type: FAX

problems.constraints: Runtime registration must work without modifying the factory class.
problems.editorial: The Registry pattern extends Factory: instead of hardcoded switch/if, new types are registered at startup or runtime. The factory is now Open for Extension without modification — adding WEBHOOK required zero changes to NotificationFactory. Spring uses this exact pattern: BeanDefinition registry, message converter registry, HandlerMapping registry.

problems.title: Builder for Complex Request Object
problems.description: Build a `DatabaseQuery` using the Builder pattern. Fields: `table` (required), `columns` (default: *), `whereClause` (optional), `orderBy` (optional), `limit` (default: 100), `offset` (default: 0). `build()` validates that `table` is not blank. `toSQL()` generates a SQL SELECT string.
problems.hint1: Put all fields and their defaults in the `Builder` class. `DatabaseQuery` has a private constructor taking the Builder.
problems.hint2: `toSQL()`: `SELECT {columns} FROM {table} [WHERE ...] [ORDER BY ...] LIMIT {limit} OFFSET {offset}`.
problems.hint3: Fluent methods return `this` for chaining: `public Builder where(String clause) { this.where = clause; return this; }`

testCases.output: SELECT id,name,email FROM users WHERE age > 18 ORDER BY name ASC LIMIT 50 OFFSET 0

problems.constraints: table is mandatory. All other fields have defaults.
problems.editorial: Builder shines when objects have many optional parameters. Without it you'd need constructors like DatabaseQuery(table, *, null, null, 100, 0) where null parameters are confusing and order-sensitive. The Builder makes the code self-documenting — .where("age > 18") is clear; the third null in a constructor is not. Lombok's @Builder annotation generates this boilerplate automatically.

problems.title: Decorator Chain — Logging and Caching
problems.description: Implement a `DataRepository` interface with `findById(int id)` returning `String`. Create: `RealRepository` (returns 'data-N', simulates 100ms delay), `CachingDecorator` (caches results, skips delay on repeat), `LoggingDecorator` (logs every call with timing). Stack them: Logging wraps Caching wraps Real.
problems.hint1: Both decorators implement `DataRepository` and take a `DataRepository` in their constructor.
problems.hint2: `CachingDecorator`: store `Map<Integer, String>`. On cache hit, return immediately. On miss, call wrapped.findById(id) and store result.
problems.hint3: `LoggingDecorator`: record `System.currentTimeMillis()` before and after the wrapped call, print the elapsed time.

testCases.output:
findById(1) = data-1 [~100ms]
findById(1) = data-1 [~0ms]
findById(2) = data-2 [~100ms]

problems.constraints: Decorator stacking order determines behaviour. Logging must be the outermost.
problems.editorial: Decorator order matters: LoggingDecorator → CachingDecorator → RealRepository. Logging wraps Caching, so the logged time includes cache lookup (fast) or real fetch (slow) — which is what you want to measure. If you swapped them (Caching wraps Logging), logging would always show the real fetch time even on cache hits. This is why Spring's @Cacheable and @Transactional order of application is significant.

problems.title: Proxy — Lazy Loading
problems.description: Implement a `HeavyReport` class that takes 500ms to load. Create a `LazyReportProxy` that implements the same `Report` interface but only loads the real report on first `getContent()` call. Subsequent calls return the cached result. Verify the report is NOT loaded on proxy creation.
problems.hint1: The proxy holds a `HeavyReport real = null` field — null until first access.
problems.hint2: On `getContent()`: `if (real == null) { real = new HeavyReport(); } return real.getContent();`
problems.hint3: Add a print in HeavyReport's constructor to verify when loading actually happens.

testCases.output:
Creating proxy...
Proxy created — title: Annual Report 2024
Report NOT loaded yet!
Getting content...
[HeavyReport] Loading...
[HeavyReport] Loaded!
Annual Report 2024 — ...
Getting content again (cached)...
Annual Report 2024 — ...

problems.constraints: HeavyReport constructor must NOT run until getContent() is first called.
problems.editorial: Lazy loading proxy: defer expensive initialisation until actually needed. getTitle() works without triggering the load — the proxy knows the title statically. Only getContent() needs the real object. This is exactly how Hibernate's lazy-loaded @ManyToOne works: the proxy knows the ID immediately, but hitting a field triggers a DB query. In multi-threaded contexts, synchronise the null check to avoid double initialisation.

problems.title: Abstract Factory — UI Theme System
problems.description: Implement an Abstract Factory for creating themed UI components. `UIFactory` creates `Button` and `TextField`. Two concrete factories: `DarkThemeFactory` and `LightThemeFactory`. Each produces components that render with their theme. The client code works with the `UIFactory` interface — it never knows which theme it's using.
problems.hint1: Interface: `UIFactory { Button createButton(String label); TextField createTextField(String placeholder); }`
problems.hint2: Each product has an interface (`Button`, `TextField`) and two implementations (`DarkButton`, `LightButton`, etc.).
problems.hint3: Client method: `void buildLoginForm(UIFactory factory)` — creates button and textfield using the factory, renders them.
problems.constraints: Client code (buildLoginForm) must not reference any concrete theme class.
problems.editorial: Abstract Factory ensures product families are consistent — you can't accidentally mix a DarkButton with a LightTextField when using a single factory instance. The factory is the only place that mentions concrete classes. This is how look-and-feel theming works in Swing (UIManager), macOS vs Windows component sets, and cross-platform toolkits.

problems.title: Observer — Event Bus
problems.description: Implement a generic `EventBus<T>` supporting: `subscribe(String eventType, Consumer<T> handler)`, `unsubscribe(String eventType, Consumer<T> handler)`, `publish(String eventType, T event)`. Support multiple handlers per event type. Demonstrate with ORDER_PLACED and ORDER_SHIPPED events.
problems.hint1: Use `Map<String, List<Consumer<T>>>` to store handlers per event type.
problems.hint2: `publish(type, event)`: get the list for that type, call each consumer.
problems.hint3: `unsubscribe`: remove the specific Consumer from the list for that type.

testCases.output:
Email to Alice for order ORD-001
SMS to Alice...
Stock reserved...

Email to Bob for order ORD-002
Stock reserved for ORD-002

problems.constraints: publish() on unknown event type does nothing. Unsubscribing a non-subscribed handler is a no-op.
problems.editorial: Publishing a copy of the list (new ArrayList<>(list)) prevents ConcurrentModificationException if a handler unsubscribes itself during event handling. computeIfAbsent creates the list on first subscription without explicit null checks. This is exactly how Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher works, and how Guava EventBus is structured.

problems.title: Strategy — Payment Processor
problems.description: Implement a `PaymentProcessor` that uses a Strategy pattern to support multiple payment methods: `CreditCard`, `PayPal`, `Crypto`. Each strategy has `pay(double amount)` returning a `PaymentResult(success, transactionId, message)`. The processor selects the strategy at runtime and applies a 2% fee for credit card, 1% for PayPal, 0% for Crypto.
problems.hint1: Interface: `PaymentStrategy { PaymentResult pay(double amount); String name(); }`
problems.hint2: Each implementation computes the fee internally or the processor applies the fee before calling pay.
problems.hint3: `PaymentProcessor` has a `setStrategy(PaymentStrategy)` method and `processPayment(double amount)` that delegates.
problems.constraints: Adding a new payment method requires only a new class — zero changes to PaymentProcessor.
problems.editorial: Strategy eliminates the if/switch in the processor — instead of if(type=='CC') charge2% else if(type=='PP') charge1%, each strategy knows its own fee. Adding SEPA bank transfer requires only one new class. The processor is closed for modification. This is how Spring's payment integrations, sorting algorithms in Collections, and compression strategies in ZIP libraries work.

problems.title: Command — Undo/Redo Text Editor
problems.description: Implement a simple text editor using Command pattern with full undo/redo. Commands: `InsertCommand(position, text)`, `DeleteCommand(position, length)`. The editor maintains undo and redo stacks. After undo, the command goes onto the redo stack. Redo reapplies the last undone command.
problems.hint1: Two stacks: `Deque<Command> undoStack` and `Deque<Command> redoStack`.
problems.hint2: `execute(cmd)`: cmd.execute(); push to undoStack; clear redoStack (a new action invalidates redo history).
problems.hint3: `undo()`: pop from undoStack, call cmd.undo(), push to redoStack. `redo()`: pop from redoStack, call cmd.execute(), push to undoStack.

testCases.input: insert Hello, insert World, undo, redo

problems.constraints: Redo stack must be cleared when a new command is executed.
problems.editorial: Undo/redo requires two stacks. New execute clears redo because a new branch of history starts. The Delete command must save the deleted text at execute() time for undo — not at construction time (position might have changed). This exact pattern is used in IDEs, word processors, and database transaction logs.

problems.title: Template Method — Data Export Pipeline
problems.description: Create a `DataExporter` abstract class with a template method `export(List<Map<String,String>> data)`. Steps: `validate(data)` (throws if empty), `format(data)` (abstract — returns List<String>), `writeHeader()` (abstract), `writeRows(rows)`, `writeFooter(rows.size())`. Implement `CSVExporter` and `JSONExporter`.
problems.hint1: `export()` is `final` — it calls the steps in order. Only `format()`, `writeHeader()` are abstract.
problems.hint2: CSVExporter: header = column names comma-joined, each row = values comma-joined.
problems.hint3: JSONExporter: header = '[', each row = '{"k":"v",...}', footer = ']'.
problems.constraints: export() must be final. validate() must be private (not overridable). format() and writeHeader() are abstract.
problems.editorial: Template Method: the base class owns the algorithm sequence (validate → format → header → rows → footer). Subclasses only fill in the blanks — they cannot reorder steps. final prevents subclasses from breaking the contract. This is the pattern behind JdbcTemplate (you provide the RowMapper), AbstractBatchJob (you implement step logic), and JUnit lifecycle hooks.

problems.title: Chain of Responsibility — HTTP Middleware
problems.description: Build an HTTP request processing chain with four handlers: `AuthMiddleware` (rejects if no 'token' header), `RateLimitMiddleware` (allows max 3 requests per client), `LoggingMiddleware` (logs request), `RouteHandler` (final handler, returns '200 OK'). Chain them and test with valid and invalid requests.
problems.hint1: Each handler has a `handle(Request req)` method and a `next` handler reference.
problems.hint2: `Request` has `path`, `headers Map<String,String>`, `clientId`. `Response` has `status`, `body`.
problems.hint3: If a handler approves, it calls `next.handle(req)`. If it rejects, it returns a Response immediately without calling next.
problems.constraints: Chain must stop at the first rejection — downstream handlers must NOT be called.
problems.editorial: Chain of Responsibility: each middleware decides independently whether to reject or pass. The order matters: Auth before RateLimit (no point rate-limiting unauthenticated requests). LoggingMiddleware is last-before-route so it can log the final response status. Spring Security's FilterChain and Spring MVC's HandlerInterceptor follow this exact pattern.

problems.title: State Machine — Order Lifecycle
problems.description: Implement an `Order` state machine with states: `PENDING → PAID → PROCESSING → SHIPPED → DELIVERED`. Each state allows only specific transitions. Invalid transitions throw `IllegalStateException`. Implement state-specific behaviour: PAID computes delivery estimate, SHIPPED records tracking number.
problems.hint1: Interface: `OrderState { void pay(Order); void process(Order); void ship(Order, String trackingNo); void deliver(Order); String stateName(); }`
problems.hint2: Each state throws IllegalStateException for invalid transitions and delegates valid ones to the next state.
problems.hint3: `Order` holds a `currentState` field. Each action delegates: `void pay() { currentState.pay(this); }`

testCases.output:
ORD-001 [PENDING]
ORD-001 [PAID]
ORD-001 [PROCESSING]
ORD-001 [SHIPPED] tracking=FEDEX-123456
ORD-001 [DELIVERED]
Error: Cannot pay when order is DELIVERED

problems.constraints: Invalid transitions throw IllegalStateException. Valid transitions update state and print action.
problems.editorial: State pattern eliminates a giant switch(currentState) in every method. Each state class is responsible for its own valid transitions — adding a CANCELLED state only requires a new CancelledState class and updating the states that can transition to it. This is how Spring Statemachine works and how workflow engines (Activiti, Camunda) model business processes.

problems.title: Composite — File System Tree
problems.description: Implement a file system using Composite: `FileSystemItem` interface with `getName()`, `getSize()`, `print(String indent)`. `File` is a leaf with a fixed size. `Directory` is a composite containing items. `getSize()` for Directory returns the sum of all children's sizes recursively. Build a sample tree and print it.
problems.hint1: `Directory` holds `List<FileSystemItem> children`. `addItem(item)` appends to the list.
problems.hint2: `getSize()` in Directory: `children.stream().mapToLong(FileSystemItem::getSize).sum()`
problems.hint3: `print(indent)` in Directory: print own name, then call `child.print(indent + "  ")` for each child.
problems.constraints: getSize() on Directory must sum recursively — not just direct children.
problems.editorial: Composite lets you treat individual items and compositions uniformly through FileSystemItem. The key insight: Directory.getSize() calls getSize() on each child — if the child is a Directory, it recurses; if it's a File, it returns directly. The caller never needs to check if something is a File or Directory. This is how Java's Path API works, and how HTML DOM traversal operates.

problems.title: Flyweight Pool — Particle System
problems.description: A particle system renders thousands of particles. Each particle has: `color`, `shape`, `texture` (shared — flyweight) and `x`, `y`, `velocity` (unique per particle). Implement a `ParticleFactory` that pools particle types. Show memory savings: 10,000 particles with only 5 unique types use 5 flyweight objects instead of 10,000.
problems.hint1: `ParticleType` record holds color, shape, texture (intrinsic state). `ParticleFactory` pools these.
problems.hint2: `Particle` holds a `ParticleType` reference (shared) plus x, y, velocity (extrinsic — unique).
problems.hint3: Factory: `Map<String, ParticleType> pool`. Key = `color+"|"+shape+"|"+texture`.

testCases.output:
Total particles:      10000
Unique particle types: 5
Memory saving: 99.9%

problems.constraints: ParticleType objects must be created at most once per unique (color, shape, texture) combination.
problems.editorial: Flyweight separates intrinsic state (shared — same for all particles of a type) from extrinsic state (unique per particle). computeIfAbsent creates on first request and reuses thereafter. 10,000 particles with 5 types: 5 ParticleType objects instead of 10,000 — 99.95% fewer intrinsic objects. Java's String.intern(), Integer cache (-128 to 127), and the JVM's constant pool are all flyweight implementations.

problems.title: Prototype — Deep Clone Registry
problems.description: Implement a `ShapeRegistry` that stores prototype shapes. `clone(String type)` returns a deep copy of the registered prototype. Shapes: `Circle(radius, color)`, `Rectangle(width, height, color)`. After cloning, modifying the clone must not affect the prototype. Demonstrate with a shape catalog.
problems.hint1: Each shape implements `Cloneable` and overrides `clone()` to return a deep copy.
problems.hint2: `ShapeRegistry`: `Map<String, Shape> prototypes`. `register(name, shape)`, `clone(name)` calls `prototype.clone()`.
problems.hint3: Java's `Object.clone()` is a shallow copy — for mutable fields (like a List of points), you must manually deep copy them.

testCases.output:
c1 after modify: Circle(r=10.0, color=red...)
c2 unchanged:    Circle(r=5.0, color=red...)
prototype:       Circle(r=5.0, color=red...)

problems.constraints: Clones must be completely independent — modifying a clone must not affect the prototype or other clones.
problems.editorial: Prototype avoids expensive initialisation by copying an existing object. The clone() method must do a DEEP copy — for reference fields, you must clone them too. Java's Object.clone() is shallow — a Shape with a List of points would share the same List between prototype and clone. Always implement clone() explicitly. Spring's BeanDefinition copying uses prototype-like semantics when creating prototype-scoped beans.

problems.title: Facade — Microservice Client Facade
problems.description: Implement a `CheckoutFacade` that coordinates three subsystems: `InventoryService.reserve(productId, qty)`, `PaymentService.charge(customerId, amount)`, `ShippingService.schedule(orderId, address)`. If payment fails, it must release the inventory reservation. Return an `OrderConfirmation` on success.
problems.hint1: Each service method can throw a checked `ServiceException`. Catch payment failure, call inventory.release().
problems.hint2: Return `OrderConfirmation(orderId, estimatedDelivery)` on full success.
problems.hint3: Use `UUID.randomUUID()` for orderId. Simulate failures with boolean flags in the service constructors.

testCases.output:
[Inventory] Reserved 1...
[Inventory] Released 1...
Error: Checkout failed: Payment declined

problems.constraints: On payment failure, inventory reservation must be released (compensating transaction).
problems.editorial: Facade hides the complexity of coordinating three services. The critical addition here is error compensation (Saga pattern): if payment fails after inventory is reserved, we must release the reservation to avoid stock being stuck. Without Facade, every checkout code path would need to implement this compensation logic. With Facade, it's centralised in one place — the single source of truth for the checkout flow.

problems.title: Abstract Factory — Cross-Platform Dialogs
problems.description: Extend the Abstract Factory to support three OS themes: Windows, macOS, Linux. Each creates a `Dialog` component (modal/modeless) and a `Menu` component (menubar/contextmenu). A `Application` class takes a `GUIFactory` and builds its UI using only the factory — never mentioning concrete classes.
problems.hint1: Three factories: `WindowsFactory`, `MacFactory`, `LinuxFactory` each implementing `GUIFactory { Dialog createDialog(); Menu createMenu(); }`
problems.hint2: Application constructor: `Application(GUIFactory factory)`. `buildUI()` calls factory methods.
problems.hint3: Select factory based on OS: `System.getProperty("os.name")` — or accept it as a config string.

testCases.output:
[Win MenuBar] [File, Edit, Help]
[Win Dialog] Welcome: Application started

[Mac Menu Bar ☰] [File, Edit, Help]
[Mac Sheet] Welcome: Application started

problems.constraints: Application class must not contain any instanceof checks or OS-specific code.
problems.editorial: Abstract Factory ensures an entire product family is consistent. You cannot mix WinDialog with MacMenu — the factory produces a complete set. The Application is completely decoupled from concrete implementations — swap the factory and the entire look-and-feel changes. This is how Java Swing's LookAndFeel works and how database drivers are loaded via JDBC — DriverManager is the abstract factory, and each driver (MySQL, PostgreSQL) is a concrete factory.

problems.title: Decorator — Input Validation Pipeline
problems.description: Build a validation pipeline using Decorator. `Validator<T>` interface has `ValidationResult validate(T input)`. Decorators: `NotNullValidator`, `MinLengthValidator(n)`, `MaxLengthValidator(n)`, `RegexValidator(pattern)`, `TrimmedValidator` (trims then delegates). Chain them to validate a username string.
problems.hint1: `ValidationResult record(boolean valid, String errorMessage)` — use static factory methods `ok()` and `error(msg)`.
problems.hint2: Each decorator validates its rule first. If it fails, return error immediately. If it passes, delegate to wrapped validator.
problems.hint3: Chain: `new RegexValidator(new MaxLengthValidator(20, new MinLengthValidator(3, new NotNullValidator())), "[a-zA-Z0-9_]+")`
problems.constraints: Each decorator checks its own rule and delegates to inner validator first — fail fast on any violation.
problems.editorial: The decorator order determines what gets checked when. TrimmedValidator wraps everything — it normalises input before any other check sees it. Then inner validators run from innermost (NotNull) outward (MinLength → MaxLength → Regex). Fail-fast: each decorator checks the inner result first, returns immediately if already invalid. This is how Bean Validation (@NotNull, @Size, @Pattern annotations) works in Spring — multiple validators compose on a single field.

problems.title: Builder With Validation and Immutability
problems.description: Implement an immutable `User` class with Builder. Fields: `id` (UUID, auto-generated), `username` (3-20 chars, required), `email` (valid format, required), `age` (18-120, required), `roles` (Set<String>, default empty), `bio` (optional, max 500 chars). `build()` must validate ALL fields and report ALL errors (not just first).
problems.hint1: Collect all validation errors in a `List<String>`. Throw `IllegalArgumentException` with all errors joined by newline.
problems.hint2: Email validation: simple regex `^[\\w._%+-]+@[\\w.-]+\\.[a-zA-Z]{2,}$`.
problems.hint3: `roles` should be deep-copied into an unmodifiable Set in the User constructor.

testCases.output:
Errors:
username must be 3-20 chars
email is invalid
age must be 18-120

problems.constraints: Report ALL validation errors, not just the first. User is immutable after build().
problems.editorial: Collecting all validation errors before throwing gives better UX — the user sees all problems at once instead of fix-one-reload-see-another. This is the same approach as Bean Validation's ConstraintViolationException which collects all @NotNull, @Size, @Email violations together. Immutability after build() is enforced by: (1) private constructor, (2) final fields, (3) unmodifiable collections, (4) no setters.

problems.title: Design Pattern Identifier
problems.description: Given code snippets, identify which design pattern is being used and explain why. Implement a `PatternAnalyzer` that stores pattern descriptions and matches code characteristics to patterns. Then implement examples of all three pattern categories (one each) in a single demo class showing Creational + Structural + Behavioral patterns working together in a real scenario: a plugin-based report generation system.
problems.hint1: System: ReportFactory (Factory Method) creates report generators. LoggingDecorator wraps them. Observers are notified when reports complete.
problems.hint2: ReportGenerator interface: `generate(data)`. Implementations: CSVGenerator, JSONGenerator, PDFGenerator.
problems.hint3: Combine: Factory creates the right generator. Decorator wraps with logging. Observer notifies audit and email systems on completion.
problems.constraints: Adding a new report format (XML) requires only registering a new factory entry — zero changes to ReportService, LoggingDecorator, or EventBus.
problems.editorial: This demo shows how patterns compose: Factory creates the right generator (Creational), Decorator wraps it with logging without subclassing (Structural), Observer decouples the notification logic from report generation (Behavioral). Adding XML is purely additive — one factory.register() call. This is the essence of Open/Closed Principle that all three patterns enable: the system is open for extension (new formats, new observers) but closed for modification (ReportService never changes).
